items.q: If voltage is doubled and resistance is unchanged, what happens to the current?
items.correct: Current doubles (V = IR, so I = V/R).
items.why: Students get the direction of proportionality wrong. Current is PROPORTIONAL to voltage (when R is constant) - more push, more flow. They sometimes confuse this with current being inversely proportional to resistance.

items.q: What property do shape memory alloys have?
items.correct: They return to a pre-set shape when heated.
items.misconception: They remember every shape they have ever been bent into.
items.why: Students think SMAs are infinitely programmable. They actually have ONE 'remembered' shape, set during high-temperature treatment. Heating restores that shape; the alloy doesn't store a history of deformations.

items.correct: Designing products usable by the widest possible range of people without special adaptation.
items.random_wrong: Adding multiple language options to packaging.
items.why: Students conflate inclusive with specialist design. Inclusive design widens MAINSTREAM products to work for more people - the OXO Good Grips kitchen tools, designed for arthritic hands, work better for everyone.
Correct answer: Fitting design to people. “Maths only” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
It's Only 5% are bigger. “95% are bigger” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What percentile range do designers typically cover (lower bound)?
items.why: Exact answer: 5. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
